```json
{
  "diagnosis": "This job exhibits severe I/O inefficiency due to extremely small access granularity. With 819,200 total I/O operations (409,600 reads + 409,600 writes) transferring only 26.2MB total data, the average transfer size is just 64 bytes per operation. This creates massive syscall overhead - the job spends 19.16% of its time on metadata operations rather than actual data transfer. The 819,144 unaligned accesses further compound the problem. At 29.1 MB/s bandwidth, this job is severely underperforming due to the overhead of making nearly a million tiny I/O calls.",

  "recommendations": [
    {
      "priority": 1,
      "bottleneck_dimension": "access_granularity",
      "explanation": "The job makes 819,200 I/O operations with 64-byte average transfer size, causing excessive syscall overhead. Each tiny write/read incurs full system call latency. Buffering these small operations into 1MB chunks will dramatically reduce syscall count from ~819K to ~26 calls.",
      "code_before": "for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */",
      "code_after": "/* Buffer small writes, flush in large chunks */\nmemcpy(big_buf + offset, buf, 64); offset += 64;\nif (offset >= 1048576) { write(fd, big_buf, offset); offset = 0; }",
      "expected_speedup": "10-50x based on KB evidence showing dramatic improvement when moving from small to large transfer sizes",
      "kb_citation": "ior_ior_small_posix_t64_n4_r1_0, ior_ior_small_posix_t64_n4_r3_2, ior_ior_small_posix_t64_n4_r2_1",
      "confidence": "high",
      "api_change": "POSIX write buffering strategy - same API, different usage pattern"
    }
  ],
  "overall_expected_improvement": "10-50x speedup range based on benchmark evidence from similar small-transfer workloads"
}
```
system_prompt:
You are an HPC I/O performance expert. You analyze Darshan profiling data
and provide specific, actionable code-level optimization recommendations.

RULES:
1. Every recommendation MUST be grounded in the benchmark evidence provided.
2. Include specific before/after code snippets showing the exact change.
3. Cite the KB entry ID for each recommendation.
4. Quantify expected improvement using benchmark measurements only.
5. Do NOT fabricate performance numbers or API calls that don't exist.
6. Prioritize recommendations by expected impact (highest speedup first).
7. Use standard HPC I/O APIs: POSIX (read/write), MPI-IO (MPI_File_*), HDF5 (H5D*).
user_prompt:
Analyze this HPC job's I/O behavior and provide code-level optimization recommendations.

## Detected Bottlenecks (ML classifier, Micro-F1=0.923):
  - **access_granularity** (confidence=1.00): I/O operations with very small transfer sizes (<1MB), causing excessive syscall overhead

## Key Contributing Features (SHAP per-label attribution):

  access_granularity:
    - POSIX_FILE_NOT_ALIGNED = 819144.0000 (|SHAP|=3.3950, increases_risk)
    - POSIX_ACCESS1_COUNT = 819200.0000 (|SHAP|=1.7656, increases_risk)
    - POSIX_WRITES = 409600.0000 (|SHAP|=1.4227, increases_risk)
    - has_apmpi = 0.0000 (|SHAP|=1.0551, decreases_risk)
    - POSIX_READS = 409600.0000 (|SHAP|=0.8281, increases_risk)


## Benchmark Evidence (verified Knowledge Base with source code):

  --- KB Entry 1 (ID: ior_ior_small_posix_t64_n4_r1_0) ---
  Benchmark: ior | Scenario: ior_small_posix_t64_n4_r1
  Bottleneck: access_granularity
  Source: https://github.com/hpc/ior (C)
  Code (POSIX): write(fd, buf, transferSize)  /* src/aiori-POSIX.c:250 */
  Code (MPIIO_indep): MPI_File_write(fh, buf, transferSize, MPI_BYTE, &status)  /* src/aiori-MPIIO.c:200 */
  Cause: I/O operations with very small transfer sizes (<1MB), causing excessive system call overhead
  Fix: Increase transfer/buffer size to >=1MB. For POSIX: aggregate small writes into large buffers. For MPI-IO: use collective I/O with larger datatypes.
  Code BEFORE:
    for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */
  Code AFTER:
    /* Buffer small writes, flush in large chunks */
memcpy(big_buf + offset, buf, 64); offset += 64;
if (offset >= 1048576) { write(fd, big_buf, offset); offset = 0; }

  --- KB Entry 2 (ID: ior_ior_small_posix_t64_n4_r3_2) ---
  Benchmark: ior | Scenario: ior_small_posix_t64_n4_r3
  Bottleneck: access_granularity
  Source: https://github.com/hpc/ior (C)
  Code (POSIX): write(fd, buf, transferSize)  /* src/aiori-POSIX.c:250 */
  Code (MPIIO_indep): MPI_File_write(fh, buf, transferSize, MPI_BYTE, &status)  /* src/aiori-MPIIO.c:200 */
  Cause: I/O operations with very small transfer sizes (<1MB), causing excessive system call overhead
  Fix: Increase transfer/buffer size to >=1MB. For POSIX: aggregate small writes into large buffers. For MPI-IO: use collective I/O with larger datatypes.
  Code BEFORE:
    for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */
  Code AFTER:
    /* Buffer small writes, flush in large chunks */
memcpy(big_buf + offset, buf, 64); offset += 64;
if (offset >= 1048576) { write(fd, big_buf, offset); offset = 0; }

  --- KB Entry 3 (ID: ior_ior_small_posix_t64_n4_r2_1) ---
  Benchmark: ior | Scenario: ior_small_posix_t64_n4_r2
  Bottleneck: access_granularity
  Source: https://github.com/hpc/ior (C)
  Code (POSIX): write(fd, buf, transferSize)  /* src/aiori-POSIX.c:250 */
  Code (MPIIO_indep): MPI_File_write(fh, buf, transferSize, MPI_BYTE, &status)  /* src/aiori-MPIIO.c:200 */
  Cause: I/O operations with very small transfer sizes (<1MB), causing excessive system call overhead
  Fix: Increase transfer/buffer size to >=1MB. For POSIX: aggregate small writes into large buffers. For MPI-IO: use collective I/O with larger datatypes.
  Code BEFORE:
    for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */
  Code AFTER:
    /* Buffer small writes, flush in large chunks */
memcpy(big_buf + offset, buf, 64); offset += 64;
if (offset >= 1048576) { write(fd, big_buf, offset); offset = 0; }


## Job Darshan Summary:
  nprocs: 4.0
  runtime_seconds: 0.7479
  POSIX_BYTES_WRITTEN: 26214400.0
  avg_write_size: 64.0
  small_io_ratio: 1.0
  seq_write_ratio: 1.0
  metadata_time_ratio: 0.1916
  total_bw_mb_s: 29.1056

## Task:
1. Explain what I/O problems this job has (grounded in Darshan values).
2. For each detected bottleneck, provide a specific code-level fix with before/after code.
3. Estimate expected improvement based ONLY on benchmark evidence (cite KB entry IDs).
4. Prioritize by expected impact.

  "diagnosis": "plain language explanation of I/O problems",
